speaker: interviewer
text: This testimony is given by Cissie Nathan. And this is tape--

speaker: interviewer
text: --six. Mrs. Nathan, before we show the pictures of your family and of your parents, I understand you just celebrated your 90th birthday. Can you tell us a little bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was 90 on the 8th of November this year. And I had said to my son and daughter-in-law I did not want a party, because it hadn't been such a good year. I was in England for tests. I'm OK, thank the Lord. But while I was there, my brother, my youngest brother, 80, died. He had a stroke and never recovered. Then, I have a sister in London who's in a home. She has Parkinson's Disease. And I see her every time I'm in England and write to her regularly. Terry came with me, and we went to see her. She had deteriorated so much. She lost weight. She did not talk. She doesn't talk anymore. She didn't recognize me. So it was a bad day. We put her in a wheel chair, because she cannot move herself. They lift her from one place to another, do everything for her. They've very good. We thought if we put her in a wheel chair and take her out-- there's big park there-- take her for a walk, she might come back. Because I spoke to the nurse who looks after her, and she said sometimes she recollects something, and then she's lost again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did that. We took her. But, no, it didn't come back. And I was in such a state.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to Terry, I can't stay. I can't bear to see here like that. So we left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was not a good year. So I said to my son and my daughter-in-law, I don't want a party. So of course, it was so much unexpected. On Sunday the 12th, they told me that we'd been invited to friends' for coffee. So I got dressed and got ready. And then Maurice put me in the car, so I thought I was going there. Then I ended up in the club house here. And there were-- I was absolutely dumb-struck-- so many people that I knew-- and some that I'd met through Terry and Maurice-- there. And what could I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the most fabulous party. My wonderful son and my wonderful daughter-in-law, because they take care of me like nobody else would-- so I had to accept it. It was most wonderful. I met a lot of people I knew and a lot of people that they knew. So it was really, really wonderful. I had 68 birthday cards. I had the most beautiful flowers and lots of gifts. So it was, in that respect, a very, very good day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also gave your son and your daughter-in-law a beautiful gift for your birthday. And that's this testimony that you gave to us. And we wish you a very happy birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you again for telling--

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you permit us, we're going to show your pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm meant to show some.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I put them? Where did I put them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it-- could you put the monitor so that she can watch the monitor instead of the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to tell you, this is a picture of my mother, Rebbecca Rabbie. Her name before her marriage was Rebbecca Clark.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in London, but I do not know the year it was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also a picture of my mother some years after my father had passed away in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. But I know it was a year when we were all working, so it must have been, I think, at least 10 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you have any idea approximately what year it was? It was made in London, you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in London, but I don't know exactly what year it was. But I know by what she's was wearing, because the necklace we'd given her once for her birthday, the children. But I don't remember what year it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father before he died. He died in London. He was 38 years old. He died of the influenza when it first came to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: He died when I was-- on my birthday-- 14th of November. And I was 14 years old. So I-- it must have been 1919.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter Helen. It was taken in London when I was visiting my mother after-- during-- before the war started. She was born in Amsterdam on the 8th of September, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she on this picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On this picture, she must have been about four or-- four or five.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son Maurice, who survived the war, taken at the same time as the one of my daughter Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This is a picture of my two children back in Amsterdam in the park. Not much later than-- about two years later than the other picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my two children. It's a school picture, and they brought them together to take this picture in the school. And it was in Amsterdam in the [? Damstraat. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my two children and my sister-in-law's little son, my nephew, who were going to a Jewish kindergarten for a few hours every morning, except the weekend, of course. My sister-in-law and I made the costumes ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: For Purim. It was a Purim party, so that's why they're wearing those costumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year this was? Give-- give us the names of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was before the last school picture. Long before that, because then they were going to ordinary school. That-- my son Maurice, my daughter-in-law Helen, and my little nephew Siegfried. He was the son of my sister-in-law, my Dutch sister-in-law. It was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Siegfried Dan, D-A-N, Thyn, T-H-Y-N. That was his name. He went with his mother and father to Birkenau-Auschwitz. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a picture of my daughter Helen that was taken by one of the British who came to the camp. She died in Belsen. They took the picture, and in England made a book called and this picture was in there. They sent me the book and several pictures. One is in the museum in Washington. And I lent the book to someone, a friend, and never received it. But must have it in their archives somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen died after the liberation of Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son Maurice. He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Raynor, now. It must've been when he was about-- I should say about 15, 16. Taken in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, yes. Sure. After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture when I was visiting my mother in England with the two children. There's just-- the other-- that's Helen and Maurice; my mother, sitting down; my sister-in-law to the right; and myself on the left. This is where my brother used to live in that area. The year, I-- I don't really know, but they were still very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- in London. We came over by boat then.

speaker: interviewer
text: She looks, like, three years old [INAUDIBLE], so it would've been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, something like that, yeah. '35, '36. Before the war, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's fine-- This is a copy of my identity that was taken in Malmo, Sweden. When I went back to Amsterdam, I had to send it-- well, we took it to the British Embassy in the Hague to verify who I was and to get a new passport. And that was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a card that everyone had to fill out, those who came out of the camps. It is a card belonging to the Allied Expeditionary Force. It shows my mother's maiden name, her married name, date of birth, and where she was born. And the card was filled in in Sweden in 1945. It's a two-sided card.
